  (c) Option 3. In lieu of a detailed stress analysis, stress indices may be used to determine peak stresses around a nozzle
opening in accordance with Annex 5-D.
   5.5.4.3 Method B — Fatigue Assessment Using Elastic–Plastic Stress Analysis and Equivalent Strain.
       5.5.4.3.1 Overview. An effective strain range is used to evaluate the fatigue damage based on the results from an
elastic–plastic stress analysis. This analysis is performed for the complete loading time history using a cyclic plasticity
algorithm with kinematic hardening. The effective strain range is subsequently calculated for each cycle identified using
a multiaxial cycle counting procedure. For simple loading time histories, the Twice Yield Method may be used in lieu of a
cycle-by-cycle analysis.
   For the calculation of the stress range and strain range of a cycle at a point in the component, a stabilized cyclic stress–
strain curve and other material properties that correspond to the cycle being evaluated for each material of construction
(i.e., material properties shall be evaluated at the average temperature corresponding to the times in the loading history)
is used. The cyclic stress–strain curve may be obtained by test for the material. Alternatively, a cyclic stress–strain curve
for a material that is known to have more conservative behavior than the actual material may be used. Cyclic stress–
strain curves are provided in Annex 3-D.4 for different materials as a function of temperature. Other cyclic stress–strain
curves that are known to be more accurate for the application or that lead to more conservative results may be used.
   A flow diagram example for the Method B Fatigue calculation procedure is shown in Figure 5.14.
      5.5.4.3.2 Assessment Procedure. The following procedure shall be used to evaluate protection against failure
due to cyclic loading using elastic–plastic stress analysis.
   Step 1. Determine a loading time history as defined in 5.1.3.3. The loading time history should include all significant
operating loads and events that are applied to the component, including planned operations, such as start-ups, shut-
downs, and emergency shutdowns.
   Step 2. Perform an elastic–plastic stress analysis of the component based on the loading time history from Step 1. The
material model used in the elastic–plastic analysis shall incorporate a cyclic plasticity algorithm with kinematic hard-
ening. The stress tensor and plastic strain tensor shall be determined at each location in the component at each time
identified in the loading time history.
   Step 3. Determine the cyclic strain ranges at the location under consideration based on the elastic–plastic stress anal-
ysis in Step 2 and the cycle counting methods in Annex 5-B (WRC 550). Define the total number of cyclic strain ranges in
the loading time history as M.
   Step 4. Perform the following for each cyclic stress range in the loading time history: k ≤ M.
   (a) Obtain the elastic strain tensor and plastic strain tensor at the start and end of the of the k th cycle counted in Step
3, m eij,k and n eij,k, and m pij,k and n pij,k, respectively.
   (b) Calculate the equivalent elastic strain range tensor, Δε e , k , for the kth cycle using eq. (5.57).
   (c) Calculate the plastic strain range tensor, Δε p e q , k , for the kth cycle using eq. (5.56).
   (d) Calculate the total equivalent effective strain range for the kth cycle using eq. (5.58).

                                                                                                                          ð5:58Þ

  (e) Calculate the effective alternating equivalent stress for the kth cycle using eq. (5.59).

                                                             607
5.5.4.3.2 – 5.5.4.4.1                               ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025




                                                                                                                         ð5:59Þ


   (f) Determine the permissible number of cycles, N k , for the alternating equivalent stress computed in (e) using a fa-
tigue curve based on the materials of construction in Annex 3-F.1 for the kth cycle.
   (g) Calculate the fatigue damage for the k th cycle using eq. (5.60) where the actual number of repetitions of the k th
cycle is n k .

                                                                                                                         ð5:60Þ

   Step 5. Calculate the accumulated fatigue damage using eq. (5.61). The location in the component is acceptable for con-
tinued operation if this equation is satisfied.

                                                                                                                         ð5:61Þ


  Step 6. Repeat Steps 3 through 5 for each point in the component subject to a fatigue evaluation.

     5.5.4.3.3 Defining the Scope of Surface Examination After Pressure Test. Surface examination after pressure
test per 7.6.1 shall be performed on any location with an accumulated damage fraction, D f , greater than 0.5, or if spe-
cified in the User’s Design Specification.

     5.5.4.3.4 The Twice Yield Method. The Twice Yield Method is an elastic–plastic stress analysis performed in a
single loading step, based on a specified stabilized cyclic stress range-strain range curve and a specified load range rep-
resenting a cycle. Stress ranges and strain ranges are the direct output from this analysis. This method is performed in
the same manner as a monotonic elastic–plastic analysis, except it does not require a cycle-by-cycle analysis of unloading
and reloading. Therefore, the Twice Yield Method may be used with an analysis program that does not have a cyclic
plasticity algorithm with kinematic hardening.
   The Twice Yield Method is based on Masing’s hypothesis that states that the stabilized hysteresis loop branch may be
obtained by doubling the basic material cyclic stress–strain curve.
   In the Twice Yield Method, the loading at the start time of the cycle is zero and the loading at the end time is the load
determined from the loading time history. Therefore, a formal cycle counting process is not used and the load range
associated with the maximum strain range needs to be deduced from the loading time history.
   The cyclic stress range versus strain range curve or hysteresis loop stress–strain curve, see Annex 3-D.4, is used in the
monotonic elastic–plastic analysis. For thermal loading, the loading range in Twice Yield Method may be applied by spe-
cifying the temperature field at the start time for the cycle as an initial condition and applying the temperature field at
the end time for the cycle in a single loading step.
   The stress tensor and plastic strain tensor are output directly from the elastic–plastic analysis and processed in ac-
cordance with 5.5.4.3.2, Steps 4 and 5.

   5.5.4.4 Method C – Fatigue Assessment of Welds Using the Equivalent Structural Stress.
      5.5.4.4.1 Overview. An equivalent structural stress range parameter is used to evaluate the fatigue damage from
the results obtained from a linear elastic stress analysis. The controlling stress for the fatigue evaluation is the structural
stress that is a function of the membrane and bending stresses normal to the hypothetical crack plane. This method is
recommended for evaluation of welded joints that have not been machined to a smooth profile. Welded joints with con-
trolled smooth profiles may be evaluated using Method A or Method B.
   Fatigue cracks at pressure vessel welds are typically located at the toe of a weld. For as-welded joints and welded
joints subject to post weld heat treatment, the expected orientation of a fatigue crack is along the weld toe in the
through-thickness direction, and the structural stress normal to the expected crack is the stress measure used to cor-
relate fatigue life data. For fillet welded components, fatigue cracking may occur at the toe of the fillet weld or the weld
throat, and both locations shall be considered in the assessment. It is difficult to accurately predict fatigue life at the weld
throat due to variability in throat dimension, which is a function of the depth of the weld penetration. It is recommended
to perform sensitivity analysis where the weld throat dimension is varied.
   If thermal transients result in a through-thickness stress difference at any time that is greater than the steady state
difference, the number of design cycles shall be determined as the smaller of the number of cycles for the base metal
established using Method A or Method B, and for the weld established using Method C.
   A flow diagram example for the Method C Fatigue calculation procedure is shown in Figure 5.15.

                                                              608
                                                    ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025                                              5.5.4.4.2



     5.5.4.4.2 Assessment Procedure. The following procedure shall be used to evaluate protection against failure
due to cyclic loading using the equivalent structural stress range. This procedure shall be performed along the weld joint
at two locations on the hypothetical crack plane, the first point corresponding to the location of maximum tensile bend-
ing stress and the second point corresponding to the location of the maximum compressive bending stress.
   Step 1. Determine a loading time history as defined in 5.1.3.3. The loading time history should include all significant
operating loads and events that are applied to the component, such as start-ups, shutdowns, and emergency shutdowns.
   Step 2. Perform an elastic stress analysis of the component based on the loading time history from Step 1. The stress
tensor shall be determined at each hypothetical crack plane in the component at each point identified in the loading time
history. Note that the normal and shear stresses on the hypothetical crack plane used in this procedure are computed
from the stress tensor.
   Step 3. Determine the cyclic stress ranges at the location under consideration based on the elastic stress analysis in
Step 2 and the cycle counting methods in Annex 5-B (WRC 550). Define the total number of cyclic stress ranges in
the loading time history as M.
   Step 4. Perform the following for each cyclic stress range in the loading time history: k ≤ M.
   (a) Calculate the elastic membrane and bending stress normal to the assumed hypothetical crack plane at the start and
end times for the kth cycle counted in Step 3. Using these data, calculate the bending stress range, and the maximum,
minimum, and mean stress for the kth cycle. The algebraic signs of the stresses shall be used in all the calculations.

5.5.5 RATCHETING ASSESSMENT
5.5.5.1 Overview
   The ratcheting assessment shall be performed using an elastic stress analysis or an elastic–plastic stress analysis. If an
elastic stress analysis is used, the results from the analysis are used with simplified assumptions to determine if shake-
down, shakedown with cyclic plasticity, or ratcheting will occur. If an elastic–plastic analysis is used to evaluate protec-
tion against ratcheting, a stress analysis is performed by application, removal, and re-application of the applied loadings
to determine the actual response of the component, i.e., to determine whether progressive inelastic incremental inelastic
deformation will occur during re-application of applied loads.

5.5.5.2      Elastic Analysis Methods — Time Independent Material Properties
   To evaluate protection against ratcheting, elastic stress analysis may be used to compute the primary and secondary
equivalent stress range and limit this value to demonstrate shakedown. In Method A, elastic stress analysis results are
used alone. In Method B, elastic stress analysis results are used in conjunction with the Bree Diagram, which is a function
of cyclic loading type. In both methods, load case combinations to be considered for typical pressure vessel components
are shown in Table 5.3.
   5.5.5.2.1 Method A — Elastic Stress Analysis.
     5.5.5.2.1.1 General Procedure. The following procedure shall be used to evaluate ratcheting using the results
from an elastic stress analysis:
   Step 1. Determine the primary plus secondary equivalent stress range, ΔS n , k from 5.5.4.2.2, Step 4(b). The primary
plus secondary equivalent stress range, ΔS n , k , is the equivalent stress range, derived from the highest value across
the thickness of a section, of the combination of linearized general or local primary membrane stress plus primary bend-
ing stress plus secondary stress Δ(P L + P b + Q ), produced by specified operating pressure and other specified mechan-
ical loads and by general thermal effects. The effects of gross structural discontinuities but not of local structural
discontinuities (stress concentrations) shall be included.
   Step 2. The allowable limit, for the kth cycle, on the primary plus secondary stress range, S P S , k is determined using eq.
(5.95). In this equation, the material parameters S y – c o l d , k and S y – h o t , k ) are evaluated at the highest and lowest tem-
perature of the k th cycle. If the material cyclically softens, then the monotonic value of the yield strength shall be derated
to account for the cyclic softening. Cyclic softening may occur in the following situations: when the ratio of the yield
strength to ultimate strength is greater than 0.7 for carbon or low alloy steels when the yield strength is greater than
50 ksi (350 MPa); in ferritic/martensitic alloys (e.g., 9-13%Cr); or in titanium alloys. In the determination of S P S , k it is
necessary to consider the effects of multiple cycles where the total stress range may be greater than the stress range of
any individual cycles. The value of S P S , k may vary with the specified cycle, or combination of cycles being considered
since the temperature extremes may be different in each case. Therefore, care shall be exercised to ensure that the ap-
plicable value of S P S , k for each cycle, or combination of cycles, is used. In some cases, the lowest and highest tempera-
ture of the kth cycle may be the same value, particularly when the cycle consists of cyclic operating pressure and other
specified operating mechanical loads at a constant temperature.

                                                                                                                               ð5:95Þ

  Step 3. If eq. (5.96) is satisfied, then protection against ratcheting is demonstrated and the ratcheting analysis is com-
plete. If eq. (5.96) is not satisfied, the component configuration shall be modified, or applied loads shall be modified, or
the Simplified Elastic–Plastic Ratcheting Analysis in 5.5.5.2.1.2 shall be satisfied, or Method B – Elastic Stress Analysis
with the Bree Diagram in 5.5.5.2.2 shall be satisfied, or the Elastic–Plastic Stress Analysis Method in 5.5.5.3 shall be
satisfied.

                                                                                                                               ð5:96Þ

     5.5.5.2.1.2 Simplified Elastic–Plastic Ratcheting Analysis Procedure. Alternatively, the simplified elastic–
plastic ratcheting analysis procedure below may be used if the vessel is in the same design and operating conditions
as a previously designed vessel analyzed using the simplified elastic–plastic analysis.

                                                                612
                                                   ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025                                 5.5.5.2.1.2 – 5.5.5.2.2



  The equivalent stress limit on the range of primary plus secondary equivalent stress ΔS n , k may exceed S P S , k [see eq.
(5.96)], provided all of the following are true, for the k th cycle:
  (a) The range of primary membrane plus primary bending plus secondary membrane equivalent stress, excluding
thermal stresses, is less than S P S , k .

                                                                                                                         ð5:97Þ

   (b) A fatigue analysis is mandatory, with the value of the alternating stress range in 5.5.4.2.2, Step 4 being multiplied
by the factor K e , k determined using eq. (5.43) through eq. (5.45), or eq. (5.55).
   (c) The material of construction for the component satisfies the specified minimum yield strength and ultimate tensile
strength ratio: SMYS/UTS ≤ 0.80.
   (d) The component meets the secondary equivalent stress range requirements of thermal stress ratcheting as outlined
in Steps 1 through 6. The allowable limit on the secondary equivalent stress range to prevent ratcheting, when applied in
conjunction with a steady state general or local primary membrane equivalent stress, is determined below. This proce-
dure can be used only with an assumed linear or parabolic distribution of a secondary stress range (e.g., thermal stress).
   Step 1. Determine the ratio of the primary membrane stress to the specified minimum yield strength from Annex
3-D.1, evaluated at the average temperature of the cycle.

                                                                                                                         ð5:98Þ


  Step 2. Compute the secondary membrane equivalent stress range, ΔQ m , using elastic analysis methods.
  Step 3. Compute the secondary membrane plus bending equivalent thermal stress range, ΔQ m b , using elastic analysis
methods.
  Step 4. Determine the allowable limit on the secondary membrane plus bending equivalent thermal stress range,
ΔS Q m b .
  (a) For a secondary equivalent thermal stress range from thermal loading with a linear variation through the wall
thickness

                                                                                                                         ð5:99Þ



                                                                                                                       ð5:100Þ

  (b) For a secondary equivalent stress range from thermal loading with a parabolic constantly increasing or decreasing
variation through the wall thickness

                                                                                                                       ð5:101Þ



                                                                                                                       ð5:102Þ

  Step 5. Determine the allowable limit on the secondary membrane equivalent thermal stress range, S Q m .

                                                                                                                       ð5:103Þ

  Step 6. To demonstrate protection against ratcheting, the following criteria shall be satisfied:

                                                                                                                       ð5:104Þ


                                                                                                                       ð5:105Þ

  5.5.5.2.2 Method B — Elastic Stress Analysis with the Bree Diagram. The use of the Bree Diagram permits alter-
nating plasticity for the primary plus secondary equivalent stress range to occur in conjunction with an applied fatigue
penalty factor, K e , k [see 5.5.4.2.2, Step 4(c)]. The procedure for evaluating ratcheting using the Bree Diagram is shown
below. This procedure considers four loading types and can only be used with a linear primary and secondary stress
range.

                                                            613
5.5.5.2.2 – 5.5.5.3.1                                    ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025



   Step 1. Determine the type of loading. The four loading types are shown below. If the loading type does not match any
of these four types, then proceed to 5.5.5.3.
   (a) Cyclic Load Type A — constant primary membrane stress, σ p , and cyclic secondary stress, σ s s (see Table 5.17 and
Figure 5.16)
   (b) Cyclic Load Type B — cyclic primary membrane stress, σ p , and cyclic secondary stress, σ s s , that are in-phase (see
Table 5.18 and Figure 5.17)
   (c) Cyclic Load Type C — cyclic primary membrane stress, σ p , and cyclic secondary stress, σ s s , that are out-of-phase
(see Table 5.19 and Figure 5.18)
   (d) Cyclic Load Type D — cyclic primary membrane stress, σ p , and constant secondary stress, σ s s (see Table 5.20 and
Figure 5.19)
   Step 2. If the Cyclic Load Type D is selected, then ratcheting cannot occur and the assessment procedure is complete.
   Step 3. Determine the yield strength at the off-load condition, typically the lowest temperature of the cycle, S y – o f f l o a d ,
and at the on-load condition, typically the highest temperature of the cycle, S y – o n l o a d , from Annex 3-D.1. Calculate the
yield strength ratio parameter, R y using eq. (5.106).

                                                                                                                               ð5:106Þ


  Step 4. Determine the ratio of the primary membrane stress to the yield strength from Annex 3-D.1, at the lowest tem-
perature of the cycle.

                                                                                                                               ð5:107Þ


  Step 5. Determine the ratio of the secondary stress to the specified minimum yield strength from Annex 3-D.1, at the
lowest average temperature of the cycle.

                                                                                                                               ð5:108Þ


   Step 6. Based on the Cyclic Load Type from Step 1, the R y parameter from Step 3, and the X and Y parameters from
Steps 4 and 5, respectively, determine the location of the point defined by the X and Y coordinates on the Bree Diagram.
   (a) If the location of the point is within the E B r e e region, the material response is elastic and ratcheting will not occur.
If the location of the point is within S 1 or S 2 regions, then ratcheting will not occur and shakedown behavior is
predicted.
   (b) If the point falls within the P 1 , P 2 , or P 3 regions, then cyclic plasticity is predicted for the primary plus secondary
equivalent stress range and a penalty factor, K e , k [see 5.5.4.2.2, Step 4(c)], is introduced into the fatigue analysis.
   (c) If the point falls within the ratcheting regions R 1 , R 2 or R 3 , then ratcheting behavior is predicted.
NOTE: The definition of the regions in the Bree diagram are as follows:

E B r e e = elastic region
     S 1 = shakedown region 1
     S 2 = shakedown region 2
     P 1 = cyclic plasticity region 1
     P 2 = cyclic plasticity region 2
     P 3 = cyclic plasticity region 3
    R 1 = ratcheting region 1
    R 2 = ratcheting region 2
    R 3 = ratcheting region 3
  Step 7. If ratcheting behavior is predicted from Step 6, then the elastic–plastic ratcheting analysis in 5.5.5.3 may be
used for the assessment of ratcheting.
  5.5.5.3 Elastic–Plastic Ratcheting Analysis Method.
     5.5.5.3.1 Overview. The protection against ratcheting is evaluated using an elastic–plastic analysis by directly si-
mulating the steady-state and cyclic loading conditions. If protection against ratcheting is satisfied, it may be assumed
that progression of the stress–strain hysteresis loop along the strain axis cannot be sustained under cyclic loading;
therefore, the hysteresis loop will stabilize. A separate check for plastic shakedown to alternating plasticity is not
required.

                                                                   614
                                                   ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025                                    5.5.5.3.2 – 5.6.1.2



      5.5.5.3.2 Assessment Procedure. The following assessment procedure shall be used to evaluate protection
against ratcheting using elastic–plastic analysis.
   Step 1. Develop a numerical model of the component including all relevant geometry characteristics. The model used
for analysis shall be selected to accurately represent the component geometry, boundary conditions, and applied loads.
   Step 2. Define all relevant loads and applicable load cases (see Table 5.1).
   Step 3. An elastic–perfectly plastic material model shall be used in the analysis. The von Mises yield function and as-
sociated flow rule shall be utilized. The yield strength defining the plastic limit shall be the minimum specified yield
strength at temperature from Annex 3-D.1. The effects of nonlinear geometry shall be included in the analysis.
   Step 4. Perform an elastic–plastic analysis for the applicable loading from Step 2 for several repetitions of a loading
event or, if more than one event is applied, of multiple events that are selected to produce the highest likelihood of ratch-
eting. If there is uncertainty relating to determination of the loading events that produce the highest likelihood of ratch-
eting, a sensitivity study is recommended.
   Step 5. The ratcheting criteria below shall be evaluated after application of a minimum of three complete repetitions of
the cycle. Additional cycles may need to be applied to demonstrate convergence. If any one of the following conditions is
met, the ratcheting criteria are satisfied. If the criteria shown below are not satisfied, the component configuration (i.e.,
thickness, or other parameters) shall be modified or applied loads reduced, and the analysis repeated.
   (a) There is no plastic action (i.e., zero plastic strains incurred) in the component.
   (b) There is an elastic core in the primary-load-bearing boundary of the component.
   (c) There is not a permanent change in the overall dimensions of the component. This may be demonstrated by devel-
oping a plot of relevant component dimensions versus time between the analyzed cycles.

5.5.5.4    Progressive Distortion of Nonintegral Connections
   Screwed-on caps, screwed-in plugs, shear ring closures, and breech lock closures are examples of nonintegral connec-
tions that are subject to failure by bell-mouthing or other types of progressive deformation. If any combination of ap-
plied loads produces yielding, such joints are subject to ratcheting because the mating members may become loose at the
end of each complete operating cycle and may start the next cycle in a new relationship with each other, with or without
manual manipulation. Additional distortion may occur in each cycle so that interlocking parts, such as threads, can even-
tually lose engagement. When evaluated using the procedure in 5.5.5.2.1, primary plus secondary equivalent stresses
that produce slippage between the parts of a nonintegral connection in which disengagement could occur as a result
of progressive distortion, shall be limited to the minimum specified yield strength at temperature, S y .


5.6     PROTECTION AGAINST CREEP DAMAGE
  Protection against creep damage shall be evaluated when the allowable stress from Annex 3-A evaluated at the design
temperature is governed by time-dependent properties.

5.6.1     PROTECTION AGAINST STRESS RUPTURE FROM LOAD-CONTROLLED LOADING
  5.6.1.1 Design-by-Rule Approach. The design of pressure parts or non-pressure parts (e.g., skirt and skirt attach-
ment) may be in accordance with the design-by-rule requirements of Part 4 using the allowable stress established from
Annex 3-A. The design is based on the most severe combination of coincident pressure, temperature and other loadings
specified in the loading time history (see 5.1.3.3) for the component.
   5.6.1.2 Elastic Stress Analysis Method. A procedure to evaluate protection against stress rupture from load-
controlled loading using elastic stress analysis is shown below. This procedure is based on the computation and cate-
gorization of equivalent stress at a location in a component, and to determine the acceptability of the resulting stress
state at each point in time of the operating cycle. Alternatively, if the controlling temperature and loading conditions
are identified, a single calculation may be used to determine acceptability.
   Step 1. The loads to be considered in the design shall include, but not be limited to, those given in Table 5.1, with the
exception of thermal loads due to temperature gradients within the component being analyzed, which do not contribute
to creep stress rupture. The load combinations that shall be considered shall include, but not be limited to, those given in
Table 5.16.
   Step 2. At the location in the vessel that is being investigated, calculate the stress tensor (six unique components of
stress) for each type of load. Assign each of the computed stress tensors to one or a group of the categories defined be-
low. Assistance in assigning each stress tensor to an appropriate category for a component may be obtained by using
Figure 5.1 and Table 5.6.
   (a) general primary membrane equivalent stress, P m
   (b) local primary membrane equivalent stress, P L

                                                            615
5.6.1.2 – 5.6.4.3                                  ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025



   (c) primary bending equivalent stress, P b
   (d) secondary equivalent stress, Q
   Step 3. Sum the stress tensors (stresses are added on a component basis) assigned to each equivalent stress category.
The final result is a stress tensor representing the effects of all the loads assigned to each equivalent stress category.
Note that in applying steps in this paragraph, a detailed stress analysis performed using a numerical method such as
finite element analysis typically provides a combination of P L + P b and P L + P b + Q directly.
   Step 4. Determine the principal stresses of the sum of the stress tensors assigned to the equivalent stress categories
and compute the equivalent stress using eq. (5.1).
   Step 5. Determine the time-dependent allowable stress, S , from Annex 3-A.
   Step 6. To evaluate protection against stress rupture from load-controlled loading, compare the computed equivalent
stress to their corresponding allowable values.
                                                                                                                     ð5:109Þ


                                                                                                                     ð5:110Þ


                                                                                                                     ð5:111Þ


                                                                                                                     ð5:112Þ


5.6.2     PROTECTION AGAINST STRESS RUPTURE FROM LOAD-CONTROLLED LOADING PLUS
          OCCASIONAL LOADING
  5.6.2.1 Design-by-Rule Approach. The design of pressure parts or non-pressure parts (e.g., skirt and skirt attach-
ment) may be in accordance with the design-by-rule requirements of Part 4 using the allowable stress established from
Annex 3-A. The design is based on the most severe combination of coincident pressure, temperature and other loadings
specified in the loading time history (see 5.1.3.3) for the component.
  5.6.2.2     Elastic Stress Analysis Method. This section is in development.

5.6.3     PROTECTION AGAINST CREEP BUCKLING
   To demonstrate protection against creep buckling, an analysis in accordance with 5.4, Method B, shall be used with the
following modifications:
   (a) In 5.4.3.20, use β b = 1.5.
   (b) In 5.4.3.20, in lieu of the elastic–plastic material model, an isochronous stress–strain curve with a time of
100 000 h and minimum properties shall be used. One method for generating an isochronous stress–strain curve is
in API 579-1/ASME FFS-1 Annex 10B.5 with ΔΩ s r = −0.5, where ΔΩ s r is the creep strain rate adjustment factor to ac-
count for the material scatter band.

5.6.4     PROTECTION AGAINST FAILURE FROM CREEP FATIGUE
   5.6.4.1 Overview. The provisions of this paragraph require that creep-fatigue interaction be avoided. To demon-
strate avoidance of creep-fatigue interaction, two screening options are given. In evaluating these options, only those
load cycles where at least one end of the cycle is in the creep regime (i.e., the allowable stress from Annex 3-A evaluated
at the design temperature is governed by time-dependent properties) are considered. If any one of the screening options
is satisfied, then a creep-fatigue analysis is not required as part of the component design. If none of the screening options
is satisfied, then a creep-fatigue evaluation is required. For load cycles occurring completely within the low-temperature,
time-independent regime, the provisions of 5.5 still apply.
  5.6.4.2 Creep-Fatigue Exemption Based on Experience With Comparable Equipment. If successful experience over
a sufficient time frame is obtained with comparable equipment subject to a similar load history and addressed in the
User’s Design Specification [see 2.2.3.1(f)], as described in 5.5.2, then creep-fatigue need not be considered as part of
the component design, and a creep-fatigue analysis is not required.
  5.6.4.3 Creep-Fatigue Screening Based on Cycles. The following procedure may be used for all materials and com-
ponents designed using Part 4 or 5.6.1.2.
  Step 1. Determine a loading time history as defined in 5.1.3.3. The loading time history shall include all cyclic operating
loads where at least one end of the cycle is in the creep regime.

                                                            616
                                                     ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025                                           5.6.4.3 – 5.7



  Step 2. Based on the loading time history in Step 1, determine the design number of full-range pressure cycles includ-
ing startup and shutdown, N Δ F P . If eq. (5.113) is satisfied, proceed to Step 4; otherwise, a creep fatigue analysis is
required.
                                                                                                                         ð5:113Þ

   Step 3. Based on the loading time history in Step 1, determine the maximum range of pressure fluctuation during nor-
mal operation, excluding startups and shutdowns, ΔP N , and the corresponding number of significant cycles, N Δ P . Sig-
nificant pressure fluctuation cycles are defined as cycles where the pressure range exceeds of the design pressure.
An example of a significant pressure fluctuation cycle for this step is given in Figure 5.9.
   Step 4. Based on the loading time history in Step 1, determine the maximum range of temperature difference fluctua-
tion, ΔT R , between any two adjacent points [see 5.5.3.3.1, Step 4 (a)] of the vessel during normal operation, excluding
startups and shutdowns, and the corresponding number of significant cycles, N Δ T R Significant temperature difference
fluctuation cycles for this step are defined as cycles where the temperature range exceeds 25% of the design tempera-
ture. An example of a significant temperature difference fluctuation cycle for this step is provided in Figure 5.10.
   Step 5. Based on the results of Steps 2, 3, and 4, evaluate eq. (5.114). If eq. (5.114) is satisfied, a detailed creep fatigue
analysis of the component is not required; otherwise, a creep fatigue analysis is required, and the provisions of this para-
graph may not be used.
                                                                                                                         ð5:114Þ


5.6.5    PROTECTION AGAINST CREEP RATCHETING
   5.6.5.1 Elastic Stress Analysis Method. The elastic stress analysis method for evaluating Protection Against Creep
Ratcheting follows the same method as described in 5.5.5.2.1. For those load cycles where at least one end of the cycle is
in the creep regime (i.e., the allowable stress from Annex 3-A evaluated at the design temperature is governed by time-
dependent properties), the limit in eq. (5.115) shall be used in lieu of the limit provided in 5.5.5.2.1.1, Step 2.

                                                                                                                         ð5:115Þ


  5.6.5.2    Elastic-Visco-Plastic Analysis Method. This section is in development.

5.6.6    SERVICE CRITERIA
   The provisions of this paragraph do not necessarily ensure that eventual unsatisfactory performance due to accumu-
lated time-dependent deformation is avoided. Satisfaction of service criteria as provided by the Owner/User shall be
demonstrated by alternate analysis methods if required. If applicable, the service criteria shall be specified in the User’s
Design Specification.


5.7     SUPPLEMENTAL REQUIREMENTS FOR STRESS CLASSIFICATION IN NOZZLE NECKS
   The following classification of stresses shall be used for stress in a nozzle neck. The classification of stress in the shell
shall be in accordance with 5.2.2.2.
   (a) Within the limits of reinforcement given by Part 4, 4.5, the following classification shall be applied whether or not
nozzle reinforcement is provided:
     (1) A P m classification is applicable to equivalent stresses resulting from pressure-induced general membrane
stresses as well as stresses, other than discontinuity stresses, due to external loads and moments including those attri-
butable to restrained free-end displacements of the attached pipe.
     (2) A P L classification shall be applied to local primary membrane equivalent stresses derived from discontinuity
effects plus primary bending equivalent stresses due to combined pressure and external loads and moments including
those attributable to restrained free-end displacements of the attached pipe.
     (3) A P L + P b + Q classification shall apply to primary plus secondary equivalent stresses resulting from a combina-
tion of pressure, temperature, and external loads and moments, including those due to restrained free-end displace-
ments of the attached pipe.
   (b) Outside of the limits of reinforcement given in Part 4, 4.5, the following classification shall be applied:
     (1) A P m classification is applicable to equivalent stresses resulting from pressure-induced general membrane
stresses as well as the average stress across the nozzle thickness due to externally applied nozzle axial, shear, and tor-
sional loads other than those attributable to restrained free-end displacement of the attached pipe.

                                                              617
5.7 – 5.8.3.1                                         ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025



      (2) A P L + P b classification is applicable to the equivalent stresses resulting from adding those stresses classified as
P m to those due to externally applied bending moments except those attributable to restrained free-end displacement of
the pipe.
      (3) A P L + P b + Q classification is applicable to equivalent stresses resulting from all pressure, temperature, and
external loads and moments, including those attributable to restrained free-end displacements of the attached pipe.
   (c) Beyond the limits of reinforcement, the S P S , k limit on the range of primary plus secondary equivalent stress may
be exceeded as provided in 5.5.5.2.1.2, except that in the evaluation of the range of primary plus secondary equivalent
stress, Δ(P L + P b + Q), stresses resulting from the restrained free-end displacements of the attached pipe may also be
excluded. The range of membrane plus bending equivalent stress attributable solely to the restrained free-end displace-
ments of the attached piping shall be less than S P S , k .


5.8     SUPPLEMENTAL REQUIREMENTS FOR BOLTS
5.8.1     DESIGN REQUIREMENTS
   (a) The number and cross-sectional area of bolts required to resist the design pressure shall be determined in accor-
dance with the procedures of Part 4, 4.16. The allowable bolt stress shall be obtained from Part 3.
   (b) When sealing is affected by a seal weld instead of a gasket, the factor for the gasket operating condition, m g a s k e t ,
and the factor for the gasket seating condition, y g a s k e t , may be taken as zero.
   (c) When gaskets are used for pre-service testing only, the design is satisfactory if the above requirements are satis-
fied for m g a s k e t and y g a s k e t factors equal to zero, and the requirements of 5.8.1 and 5.8.2 are satisfied when the appro-
priate m g a s k e t and y g a s k e t factors are used for the test gasket.

5.8.2     SERVICE STRESS REQUIREMENTS
   Actual service stress in bolts, such as those produced by the combination of preload, pressure, and differential expan-
sion, may be higher than the allowable stress values given in Annex 3-A.
   (a) The maximum value of service stress, averaged across the bolt cross section and neglecting stress concentrations,
shall not exceed two times the allowable stress values in 3-A.2.2 of Annex 3-A.
   (b) The maximum value of service stress, except as restricted by 5.8.3.1(b) at the periphery of the bolt cross section
resulting from direct tension plus bending and neglecting stress concentrations, shall not exceed 3 times the allowable
stress values in Annex 3-A, 3-A.2. When the bolts are tightened by methods other than heaters, stretchers, or other
means that minimize residual torsion, the stress measure used in the evaluation shall be the equivalent stress as defined
in eq. (5.1).

5.8.3     FATIGUE ASSESSMENT OF BOLTS
   5.8.3.1 Analysis Procedure. The suitability of bolts for cyclic operation shall be determined in accordance with the
following procedures unless the component on which they are installed meets all the conditions of 5.5.2.1 or 5.5.3 (a
fatigue analysis is not required).
   (a) Bolts made of materials that have minimum specified tensile strengths of less than 689 MPa (100,000 psi) shall be
evaluated for cyclic operation using the method in 5.5.4.2, using the applicable design fatigue curve from 3-F.1.2(a),
3-F.1.2(b), 3-F.1.2(c), or 3-F.1.2(d), and, unless it is shown by analysis or test that a lower value is appropriate, the fa-
tigue strength reduction factor used in the evaluation shall not be less than 4.0.
   (b) High strength alloy steel bolts and studs may be evaluated for cyclic operation using the methodology in 5.5.4.2
using the design fatigue curve of 3-F.1.2(e), provided all of the following are true:
     (1) The material is one of the following: SA-193 Grade B7 or B16; SA-320 Grade L43; or SA-540 Grades B23 and B24,
heat treated in accordance with Section 6 of SA-540.
     (2) The maximum value of the service stress at the periphery of the bolt cross section (resulting from direct tension
plus bending and neglecting stress concentrations) shall not exceed 2.7S, if the higher of the two fatigue design curves
for high strength bolting given in Annex 3-F is used (the 2S limit for direct tension is unchanged).
     (3) The threads shall be of a V-Type, having a minimum thread root radius no smaller than 0.076 mm (0.003 in).
     (4) The fillet radii at the ends of the shank shall be such that the ratio of fillet radius to shank diameter is not less
than 0.060.
     (5) The fatigue strength reduction factor used in the evaluation shall not be less than 4.0 unless another value is
justified.

                                                                618
                                                   ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025                                        5.8.3.2 – 5.13



5.8.3.2    Acceptance Criterion
   The bolts shall be acceptable for the specified cyclic operation application of loads and thermal stresses provided the
fatigue damage fraction, D f , is less than or equal to 1.0 (see 5.5.4).


5.9    SUPPLEMENTAL REQUIREMENTS FOR PERFORATED PLATES
  Perforated plates may be analyzed using any of the procedures in this Part if the holes are explicitly included in the
numerical model used for the stress analysis. An elastic stress analysis option utilizing the concept of an effective solid
plate is described Annex 5-E.


5.10      SUPPLEMENTAL REQUIREMENTS FOR LAYERED VESSELS
   The equations developed for solid wall cylindrical shells, spherical shells, or heads as expressed in this Part may be
applied to layered cylindrical shells, spherical shells or heads, provided the in-plane shear force on each layer is ade-
quately supported by the weld joint. In addition, consideration shall be given to the construction details in the zones
of load application. To ensure solid wall equivalence for layered cylindrical shells, spherical shells, or heads as described
above, all cylindrical shells, spherical shells, or heads subjected to radial forces and/or longitudinal bending moments
due to discontinuities or externally applied loads shall have all layers adequately bonded together to resist any longi-
tudinal shearing forces resulting from the radial forces and/or longitudinal bending moments acting on the sections.
For example, the use of the girth weld to bond layers together is shown in Figure 5.20, Figure 5.21, and Figure 5.22.
The required width of the attachment weld at the midpoint of the weld depth is given by eq. (5.116).

                                                                                                                      ð5:116Þ

  In eq. (5.116), the parameter M o is the longitudinal bending moment per unit length of circumference existing at the
weld junction of a layered cylindrical shells, spherical shells, or head. This parameter is determined from a stress anal-
ysis considering the pressure loading and all externally applied loads such as M 1 , Q 1 , and F 1 .


5.11      EXPERIMENTAL STRESS ANALYSIS
  Requirements for determining stresses in parts using experimental stress analysis are provided in Annex 5-F.


5.12      FRACTURE MECHANIC EVALUATIONS
  Fracture mechanics evaluations performed to determine the MDMT in accordance with Part 3, 3.11.2.8 shall be in ac-
cordance with API/ASME FFS-1. Residual stresses resulting from welding shall be considered along with primary and
secondary stresses in all fracture mechanics calculations.


5.13      DEFINITIONS

bending stress: the variable portion of the normal stress equal to the equivalent linear stress minus the membrane stress
when peak stresses are present or equal to the total stress minus the membrane stress in areas where no peak stresses
exist (see Figure 5.23). The equivalent linear stress is the linear stress distribution that has the same net bending mo-
ment as the actual distribution. Annex 5-A provides information on linearization techniques to determine the equivalent
linear stress distribution.
bifurcation buckling: the point of instability where there is a branch in the primary load versus displacement path for a
structure.
creep: the special case of inelasticity that characterizes the stress induced time-dependent deformation under load,
usually occurring at elevated temperatures.
creep damage: in polycrystalline materials (e.g., metals) creep damage results from the motion of dislocations within
grains, grain boundary sliding and microstructural diffusion processes within the crystalline lattice. The resultant grain
boundary voids, or grain and grain boundary distortions (damage), generally impairs the material's strain-hardening
capability.

                                                            619
5.13                                                ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025



creep rupture: an extension of the creep process to the limiting condition of gross section failure. The stress that will
cause creep fracture at a given time in a specified environment is the creep rupture strength. Also called creep fracture.
cycle: a relationship between stress and strain that is established by the specified loading at a location in a vessel or
component. More than one stress–strain cycle may be produced at a location, either within an event or in transition be-
tween two events, and the accumulated fatigue damage of the stress–strain cycles determines the adequacy for the spe-
cified operation at that location. This determination shall be made with respect to the stabilized stress–strain cycle.
cyclic loading: a service in which fatigue becomes significant due to the cyclic nature of the mechanical or thermal loads,
or both. Screening criteria are provided in 5.5.3 that may be used to determine if a fatigue analysis shall be included as
part of the component design.
elastic follow-up: a structural behavior where the elastic material surrounding a region with material nonlinearity (i.e.,
plasticity, creep, or combined plasticity and creep) results in the region experiencing loading conditions between the
extremes of load-controlled (primary) stress and strain-controlled (secondary) stress. The material nonlinearity in
the region results in a reduction in structural stiffness and subsequent strain concentration.
event: the User’s Design Specification may include one or more events that produce fatigue damage. Each event consists
of loading components specified at a number of time points over a time period and is repeated a specified number of
times. For example, an event may be the startup, the shutdown, an upset condition, or any other cyclic action. The se-
quence of multiple events may be specified or random.
fatigue: the conditions leading to fracture under repeated or fluctuating stresses having a maximum value less than the
tensile strength of the material.
fatigue Endurance Limit: the maximum stress below which a material can undergo 1011 alternating stress cycles without
failure.
fatigue strength: the maximum cyclic stress a material can withstand for a given number of cycles before failure occurs.
fatigue strength reduction factor: a stress intensification factor that accounts for the effect of a weld (specifically material
mismatch, level of inspection, weld geometry based on type of weld (e.g., butt welds and fillet welds), and weld dressing
(e.g., as-welded or machined) on the fatigue strength. The fatigue strength reduction factor is the ratio of the fatigue
strength of a component without a weld joint to the fatigue strength of that same component with a weld joint. Values
for some specific cases are empirically determined (e.g., socket welds). In the absence of experimental data, the stress
intensification factor may be developed from the guidance provided in Table 5.12 and Table 5.13. Welds need not be
modeled explicitly (nominal geometry) when using this factor.
fracture mechanics: an engineering discipline concerned with the behavior of cracks in materials. Fracture mechanics
models provide mathematical relationships for critical combinations of stress, crack size and fracture toughness that
lead to crack propagation. Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM) approaches apply to cases where crack propagation
occurs during predominately elastic loading with negligible plasticity. Elastic–Plastic Fracture Mechanics (EPFM) meth-
ods are suitable for materials that undergo significant plastic deformation during crack propagation.
gross structural discontinuity: a source of stress or strain intensification that affects a relatively large portion of a struc-
ture and has a significant effect on the overall stress or strain pattern or on the structure as a whole. Examples of gross
structural discontinuities are head-to-shell and flange-to-shell junctions, nozzles, and junctions between shells of differ-
ent diameters or thicknesses.
inelastic analysis: a stress analysis in which material nonlinearity from plasticity and creep is considered.
limit analysis: a special case of plastic analysis in which the material is assumed to be ideally plastic (non-strain-
hardening). In limit analysis the equilibrium and flow characteristics at the limit state are used to calculate the collapse
load.
limit analysis collapse load: the methods of limit analysis are used to compute the maximum load a structure made of an
ideally-plastic material can carry. The deformations of an ideally-plastic structure increase without bound at this load,
which is termed the collapse load.
local primary membrane stress: cases arise in which a membrane stress produced by pressure, or other mechanical load-
ing associated with a primary and/or a discontinuity effect would, if not limited, produce excessive distortion in the
transfer of load to other portions of the structure. Conservatism requires that such a stress be classified as a local pri-
mary membrane stress even though it has some characteristics of a secondary stress.

                                                              620
                                                     ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025                                                   5.13



local structural discontinuity: a source of stress or strain intensification that affects a relatively small volume of material
and does not have a significant effect on the overall stress or strain pattern, or on the structure as a whole. Examples are
small fillet radii, small attachments, and partial penetration welds.

membrane stress: The component of normal stress that is uniformly distributed and equal to the average value of stress
across the thickness of the section under consideration.

normal stress: the component of stress normal to the plane of reference. The distribution of normal stress is not usually
uniform through the thickness of a part.

notch effects: geometric discontinuities that typically are not able to be modeled explicitly in an analysis during the de-
sign phase (e.g., weld ripples in the as-welded condition).

operational cycle: the initiation and establishment of new conditions followed by a return to the conditions that pre-
vailed at the beginning of the cycle. Three types of operational cycles are considered: the startup-shutdown cycle, de-
fined as any cycle that has atmospheric temperature or pressure, or both, as one of its extremes and normal
operating conditions as its other extreme; the initiation of, and recovery from, any emergency or upset condition or pres-
sure test condition that shall be considered in the design; and the normal operating cycle, defined as any cycle between
startup and shutdown that is required for the vessel to perform its intended purpose.

peak stress: the basic characteristic of a peak stress is that it does not cause any noticeable distortion and is objectionable
only as a possible source of a fatigue crack or a brittle fracture. A stress that is not highly localized falls into this category
if it cannot cause noticeable distortion. Examples of peak stress include the thermal stress in the austenitic steel cladding
of a carbon steel vessel, the thermal stress in the wall of a vessel or pipe caused by a rapid change in temperature of the
contained fluid, and the stress at a local structural discontinuity.

plastic analysis: a stress analysis method where the structural behavior of a component under load is computed consid-
ering the plasticity characteristics of the material including strain hardening and stress redistribution.

plastic instability load: for a structure under predominantly tensile or compressive loading, the load at which unbounded
plastic deformation can occur without further load increase. At the plastic tensile instability load, the true stress in the
material increases faster than the strain hardening can accommodate.

plasticity: a general characterization of material behavior in which the material undergoes time independent non-
recoverable deformation.

primary stress: a normal or shear stress developed by the imposed loading that is necessary to satisfy the laws of equili-
brium of external and internal forces and moments. The basic characteristic of a primary stress is that it is not self-
limiting. Primary stresses that considerably exceed the yield strength will result in failure or at least in gross distortion.
A thermal stress is not classified as a primary stress. Primary membrane stress is divided into general and local cate-
gories. A general primary membrane stress is one that is distributed in the structure such that no redistribution of load
occurs as a result of yielding. Examples of primary stress are general membrane stress in a circular cylindrical or a sphe-
rical shell due to internal pressure or distributed live loads, and bending stress in the central portion of a flat head due to
pressure. Cases arise in which a membrane stress produced by pressure or other mechanical loading and associated
with a primary and/or a discontinuity effect would, if not limited, produce excessive distortion in the transfer of load
to other portions of the structure. Conservatism requires that such a stress be classified as a local primary membrane
stress even though it has some characteristics of a secondary stress. Finally, a primary bending stress is defined as a
bending stress developed by the imposed loading that is necessary to satisfy the laws of equilibrium of external and
internal forces and moments.

ratcheting: s progressive incremental inelastic deformation or strain that can occur in a component subjected to varia-
tions of mechanical stress, thermal stress, or both (thermal stress ratcheting is partly or wholly caused by thermal
stress). Ratcheting is produced by a sustained load acting over the full cross section of a component, in combination with
a strain controlled cyclic load or temperature distribution that is alternately applied and removed. Ratcheting causes
cyclic straining of the material, which can result in failure by fatigue and at the same time produces cyclic incremental
growth of a structure, which could ultimately lead to collapse.

secondary stress: a normal stress or a shear stress developed by the constraint of adjacent parts or by self-constraint of a
structure. The basic characteristic of a secondary stress is that it is self-limiting. Local yielding and minor distortions can
satisfy the conditions that cause the stress to occur and failure from one application of the stress is not to be expected.
Examples of secondary stress are a general thermal stress and the bending stress at a gross structural discontinuity.

                                                              621
5.13 – 5.14                                          ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025



shakedown: caused by cyclic loads or cyclic temperature distributions that produce plastic deformations in some regions
of the component when the loading or temperature distribution is applied, but upon removal of the loading or tempera-
ture distribution, only elastic primary and secondary stresses are developed in the component, except in small areas
associated with local stress (strain) concentrations. These small areas shall exhibit a stable hysteresis loop, with no in-
dication of progressive deformation. Further loading and unloading, or applications and removals of the temperature
distribution shall produce only elastic primary and secondary stresses.
shear stress: the component of stress tangent to the plane of reference.
stress concentration factor: the ratio of the maximum stress to the average section stress or bending stress considering
geometric discontinuities (excluding welds). In the absence of experimental data or when geometry is not explicitly in-
cluded in the model, a stress intensification factor is developed from a theoretical stress concentration factor derived
from the theory of elasticity. This factor is required when these discontinuities are passing through or near welds.
stress cycle: a condition in which the alternating stress difference goes from an initial value through an algebraic max-
imum value and an algebraic minimum value and then returns to the initial value. A single operational cycle may result
in one or more stress cycles.
thermal stress: a self-balancing stress produced by a non-uniform distribution of temperature or by differing thermal
coefficients of expansion. Thermal stress is developed in a solid body whenever a volume of material is prevented from
assuming the size and shape that it normally should under a change in temperature. For the purpose of establishing
allowable stresses, two types of thermal stress are recognized, depending on the volume or area in which distortion
takes place. A general thermal stress that is associated with distortion of the structure in which it occurs. If a stress
of this type, neglecting stress concentrations, exceeds twice the yield strength of the material, the elastic analysis
may be invalid and successive thermal cycles may produce incremental distortion. Therefore, this type is classified as
a secondary stress. Examples of general thermal stress are the stress produced by an axial temperature distribution
in a cylindrical shell, the stress produced by the temperature difference between a nozzle and the shell to which it is
attached, and the equivalent linear stress produced by the radial temperature distribution in a cylindrical shell. A Local
thermal stress is associated with almost complete suppression of the differential expansion and thus produces no sig-
nificant distortion. Such stresses shall be considered only from the fatigue standpoint and are therefore classified as local
stresses. Examples of local thermal stresses are the stress in a small hot spot in a vessel wall, the difference between the
nonlinear portion of a through-wall temperature gradient in a cylindrical shell, and the thermal stress in a cladding ma-
terial that has a coefficient of expansion different from that of the base metal.

       S y – o n l o a d = the yield strength at the on-load condition, typically the highest temperature of the cycle
                S C F i j = stress concentration factor applicable to the ij component of stress
                 SMYS = specified minimum yield strength at room temperature
                         T = design self-restraining load parameter (see Table 5.2)
                           t = minimum wall thickness in the region under consideration, or the thickness of the vessel, as
                               applicable

                                                    ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025                                                     5.14



                    t 1 = minimum wall thickness associated with R1
                    t 2 = minimum wall thickness associated with R2
                T a v g = constant temperature equal to the average of the temperature distribution
                   T b = the linearly varying portion of the temperature distribution
                 t e s s = structural stress effective thickness
                   T O = operating self-restraining load parameter (see Table 5.2)
                   T p = the nonlinear portion of the temperature distribution
               T ( x ) = nonlinear temperature distribution along the stress classification line, where the temperature is
                            defined as a function of x
                 UTS = minimum specified ultimate tensile strength at room temperature
                    W = wind load parameter (see Table 5.2)
                     w = required width of attachment
                W P T = pressure test wind load parameter (see Table 5.2)
                       X = ratio of the primary membrane stress to the yield strength from Annex 3-D.1, at the lowest tem-
                            perature of the cycle
                       x = distance in the through-thickness direction, ranges from 0 to t
                 X i n t = the common point on the Bree Diagrams
                    X s = ratio of the general primary membrane stress to the specified minimum yield strength from Annex
                            3-D.1, evaluated at the average temperature of the cycle
                       Y = ratio of the secondary stress to the yield strength from Annex 3-D.1, at the lowest temperature of
                            the cycle
           y g a s k e t = factor for the gasket seating condition
                      α = cone angle
                   α 1 = thermal expansion coefficient of material 1 evaluated at the mean temperature of the cycle
                   α 2 = thermal expansion coefficient of material 2 evaluated at the mean temperature of the cycle
                 α a , k = thermal expansion coefficient of the material evaluated at the mean temperature of the k th cycle
                   α b = thermal expansion coefficient of the material at the mean temperature of two adjacent points, or
                            the thermal expansion coefficient of the material evaluated at the mean temperature of the cycle, as
                            applicable
                   α s l = material factor for the multiaxial strain limit
                       β = elastic–plastic load factor used in Table 5.5 (see Table 4.1.3)
                    β b = buckling load factor used in Table 5.15
β b c o m p o n e n t , k = buckling load factor for each component for the kth combination
                  β c r = capacity reduction factor
                   β T = test load factor for hydrostatic or pneumatic test (see Table 4.1.3)
                       δ = out-of-phase angle between Δσ k and           for the kth cycle
              Δe i j , k = total strain range components minus the free thermal strain at the point under evaluation for the
                            k th cycle
                          = elastically calculated structural strain range at the point under evaluation for the k th cycle

              Δp i j , k = plastic strain range components at the point under evaluation for the k th loading condition or cycle
                 ΔP N = maximum design range of pressure associated with N Δ P
                   ΔQ = range of secondary equivalent stress
                ΔQ m = secondary membrane equivalent stress range
              ΔQ m b = secondary membrane plus bending equivalent thermal stress range
           ΔS e s s , k = equivalent structural stress range parameter for the kth cycle
            ΔS L T , k = local thermal equivalent stress range for the kth cycle

5.14                                                     ASME BPVC.VIII.2-2025



                     ΔS M L = equivalent stress range computed from the specified full range of mechanical loads, excluding pres-
                                    sure but including piping reactions
                     ΔS n , k = primary plus secondary equivalent stress range
                    ΔS P , k = range of primary plus secondary plus peak equivalent stress for the k th cycle
             ΔS P – L T , k = range of primary plus secondary plus peak stress minus the local thermal stress for the k th cycle
                           ΔT = operating temperature range
                        ΔT A = maximum range of temperature difference between any two adjacent points (see 5.5.3.30) of the
                                    vessel for the defined cycle
                     ΔT A B = temperature difference between any two adjacent points of the component at a given point in time
                        ΔT E = effective number of changes in metal temperature between any two adjacent points
                       ΔT M = the difference between the maximum and minimum operating temperatures during normal opera-
                                    tion for components fabricated from different construction materials
                       ΔT N = maximum range of temperature difference between any two adjacent points during startup and
                                    shutdown operation
                        ΔT R = the maximum range of temperature difference between any two adjacent points during normal op-
                                    eration, excluding startup and shutdown
                        ΔT T = twice the difference between the maximum and minimum temperature for the defined cycle, this is
                                    a more conservative but simpler calculation than ΔT A
                    Δε e f f , k = effective strain range for the k th cycle
                     Δε e , k = equivalent elastic strain range tensor for the k th cycle
                   Δε e l , k = equivalent strain range for the k th cycle, computed from elastic analysis
                    Δε i j , k = component strain range for the k th cycle, computed using the total strain less the free thermal
                                    strain
                         Δε k = local nonlinear structural strain range at the point under evaluation for the k th cycle
                Δε p e q , k = equivalent plastic strain range for the k th loading condition or cycle

                       ε p e q = equivalent plastic strain

              parameter definitions). Shall be 1.0 unless otherwise specified in the User’s Design Specification
              [see 2.2.3.1(e)]. The product of Ωp and P represents the maximum anticipated operating pressure
              (internal or external) acting simultaneously with occasional loads L, S s , W , or E.

5.15     TABLES



                                                      Table 5.1
                                   Loads and Load Cases to Be Considered in a Design
           Loading Condition                                                       Design Loads
   Pressure testing                      (1) Dead load of component plus insulation, fireproofing, installed internals, platforms, and other
                                             equipment supported from the component in the installed position
                                         (2) Piping loads including pressure thrust
                                         (3) Applicable live loads excluding vibration and maintenance live loads
                                         (4) Pressure and fluid loads (water) for testing and flushing equipment and piping unless a pneumatic
                                             test is specified
                                         (5) Wind loads
   Normal operation                      (1) Dead load of component plus insulation, refractory, fireproofing, installed internals, catalyst,
                                             packing, platforms, and other equipment supported from the component in the installed position
                                         (2) Piping loads including pressure thrust
                                         (3) Applicable live loads
                                         (4) Pressure and fluid loading during normal operation
                                         (5) Thermal loads
   Normal operation plus occasional      (1) Dead load of component plus insulation, refractory, fireproofing, installed internals, catalyst,
     [Note (1)]                              packing, platforms, and other equipment supported from the component in the installed position
                                         (2) Piping loads including pressure thrust
                                         (3) Applicable live loads
                                         (4) Pressure and fluid loading during normal operation
                                         (5) Thermal loads
                                         (6) Wind, earthquake, or other occasional loads, whichever is greater
                                         (7) Loads due to wave action
   Abnormal or start-up operation plus   (1) Dead load of component plus insulation, refractory, fireproofing, installed internals, catalyst,
     occasional [Note (1)]                   packing, platforms, and other equipment supported from the component in the installed position
                                         (2) Piping loads including pressure thrust
                                         (3) Applicable live loads
                                         (4) Pressure and fluid loading associated with the abnormal or start-up conditions
                                         (5) Thermal loads
                                         (6) Wind loads

   NOTE:
   (1) Occasional loads are usually governed by wind and earthquake; however, other load types such as snow and ice loads may govern;
       see ASCE/SEI-7.

                   Operating Load
  Design Load       Parameter
   Parameter         [Note (1)]                                                      Description
       P                 Po         Internal and external specified design or operating pressure
       PS                PSo        Design or operating static head from liquid or bulk materials (e.g., catalyst)
                                    Selected hydrostatic or pneumatic test pressure [see 8.2.1(d) or 8.3.1]. Used only in the pressure test load
       PT                …
                                      combinations.
                                    Deadweight of the vessel, contents, and appurtenances at the location of interest, including the following:
                                      • weight of vessel including internals, supports (e.g., skirts, lugs, saddles, and legs), and appurtenances
                                        (e.g., platforms, ladders, etc.)
                                      • weight of vessel contents design, operating, and test conditions
       D                 Do           • refractory linings, insulation
                                      • static reactions from the weight of attached equipment, such as motors, machinery, other vessels, and
                                        piping
                                      • transportation loads (the static forces obtained as equivalent to the dynamic loads experienced during

      WPT               Wpt

       Ss                Ss         Snow loads
                                    The self-restraining load case (i.e., thermal loads, applied displacements). This load case does not typically
       T                 To           affect the collapse load but shall be considered in cases where elastic follow-up causes stresses that do not
                                      relax sufficiently to redistribute the load without excessive deformation.

 (b) As an example illustrating the use of this table, consider a component subject to metal temperature differentials for the following num-
     ber of thermal cycles:

                                                                Temperature Factor Based
           Temperature Differential                             on Temperature Differential                       Number of Thermal Cycles
           28°C (50°F)                                                      0                                                1,000
           50°C (90°F)                                                      1                                                  250
           222°C (400°F)                                                   12                                                    5

       The effective number of thermal cycles due to changes in metal temperature is: N Δ T E = 1,000(0) + 250(1) + 5(12) = 310 cycles

                              Description                                       Total Stress Range Tensor                 Range Tensor
If all sources of peak stress are explicitly accounted for in the stress
   analysis model and no welds are present.
Use Option 1 for elastic–plastic analysis.
For the case of a well-defined geometric SCF, not at a weld location.
  Note SCFi j ≥ 1.0.
These equations are used for welded locations where the stress
  concentration factor effects are explicitly included in the stress
  analysis model. A fatigue strength reduction factor, K f , shall be
  included.

Recommended values for fatigue strength reduction factors for welds
  are provided in Tables 5.12 and 5.13. If other values of the fatigue
  strength reduction factors are used, they shall be applied to the
  stress consistent with their derivation.
The weld fatigue strength reduction and stress concentration effects
  are present but are not included in the stress analysis model. The
  value of K f × SCF may be limited to 5.0 unless a higher value is
  indicated by test data or experience. Other recognized methods may
  be used if their combination of method and resistance may be shown
  to be in accordance with the design margins of the applicable
  construction code.

                                           Weld Fatigue Strength Reduction Factors, K f
                                                                                   Quality Levels (See Table 5.13)
       Weld Condition         Surface Condition            1            2             3            4             5             6            7
 Full penetration           Machined                      1.0           1.5          1.5           2.0          2.5           3.0          4.0
   (including the cover     As-welded                     1.2           1.6          1.7           2.0          2.5           3.0          4.0
   fillet or cap fillet)

 Partial penetration        Final surface machined        NA            1.5          1.5           2.0          2.5           3.0          4.0

                                                           Table 5.13
                                           Weld Fatigue Strength Reduction Factors, K f
   Fatigue Strength
 Reduction Factor, K f     Quality Level                                                   Definition
          1.0                   1           Machined or ground weld that receives a full volumetric examination, and a surface that receives MT/PT
                                             examination and a VT examination

          1.2                   1           As-welded weld that receives a full volumetric examination, and a surface that receives MT/PT and VT
                                              examination

          1.5                   2           Machined or ground weld that receives a partial volumetric examination, and a surface that receives MT/
                                             PT examination and VT examination

          1.6                   2           As-welded weld that receives a partial volumetric examination, and a surface that receives MT/PT and VT
                                              examination

          1.5                   3           Machined or ground weld surface that receives MT/PT examination and a VT examination (visual), but
                                             the weld receives no volumetric examination inspection

          1.7                   3           As-welded surface that receives MT/PT examination and a VT examination (visual), but the weld receives
                                              no volumetric examination inspection

          2.0                   4           Weld has received a partial or full volumetric examination, and the surface has received VT examination,
                                             but no MT/PT examination

          2.5                   5           VT examination only of the surface; no volumetric examination nor MT/PT examination

          3.0                   6           Volumetric examination only

          4.0                   7           Weld backsides that are nondefinable and/or receive no examination

 GENERAL NOTES:
 (a) Volumetric examination is RT or UT in accordance with Part 7.
 (b) MT/PT examination is magnetic particle or liquid penetrant examination in accordance with Part 7.
 (c) VT examination is visual examination in accordance with Part 7.
 (d) See WRC Bulletin 432 for further information.
